# Literature Refresh: Topic 02 — Subtelomere Structure
## TAR1, (TTAGGG)n ITS, and the duplication-rich boundary zone
**Refresh window:** 2023–2026  
**Generated by:** lit-refresh-02-subtelomere (agent-49), 2026-05-17  
**Input bib:** `paper_prep/lit_review/topic_02_subtelomere_structure.bib`  
**Reference universe:** `paper_prep/synthesis/REFERENCES_v3.bib`

---

## Section 1: Topic scope

Topic 02 covers the structural biology of human subtelomeres as a sequence and repeat landscape: the TAR1 satellite family (a ~1.6 kb repeat present within 2 kb of the (TTAGGG)n array at ~95% of all chromosome arms), interstitial telomeric sequences (ITS; degenerate (TTAGGG)n-like arrays inside the subtelomeric zone), and the duplication-rich block architecture (11 duplicon families catalogued by Ambrosini 2007; Riethman two-domain model). In the Nature draft, this topic is directly invoked in:

- **Main paragraph 4 (L32)** — "Sixteen of nineteen arms that contain an internal (TTAGGG)n island also have an ITS within 50 kb of the inferred breakpoint, consistent with telomeric repeat fragments demarcating the proximal-to-distal transition"; also "TAR1 prevalence (Extended Data Fig. 3a)… PAR1 arms are TAR1-free (chrXp 0.3%, chrYp 1.1%), acrocentric p-arms sit at 73 to 79%, and the remaining autosomal arms saturate above 99%."
- **Extended Data Fig. 3** — TAR1 prevalence per arm, internal (TTAGGG)n island length and motif composition, terminal telomere length by community, per-arm TAR1 distance from telomere.
- **Implicit context throughout** — the case for a reference-free (C2) approach, and the explanation for the ~80–100% structural sharing at most arms (C4), both rest on the TAR1/ITS/duplicon landscape reviewed in this topic.
- **D4Z4 subtelomere** — the 4q/10q community (C1, DUX4) is specifically highlighted in the draft; its structural basis (subtelomeric D4Z4, β-satellite flanks) is topic-02 territory.

The CONSISTENCY_AUDIT_v1.md flags this topic as PARTIALLY covered: TAR1 prevalence numbers appear in the draft but ITS island motif composition (52.3% canonical / 47.7% degenerate variants) and the TERRA promoter landscape are mentioned only in figure captions, not main text.

---

## Section 2: Existing citations — status

All bibkeys are from `paper_prep/lit_review/topic_02_subtelomere_structure.bib` (`subtelstruct_*` prefix) plus cross-listed keys now in REFERENCES_v3.bib.

| Bibkey | Status | Reason |
|--------|--------|--------|
| `subtelstruct_Brown1990` | STILL CURRENT | Foundational TAR1 description; no more comprehensive structural characterisation of the original TAR1 isolates exists. |
| `subtelstruct_Wilkie1991` | STILL CURRENT | Telomere stabilisation of truncated chr16; historical, no supersession. |
| `subtelstruct_WilkiePolymorphism1991` | STILL CURRENT | First large-scale (up to 260 kb) subtelomeric length polymorphism; historical observation holds. |
| `subtelstruct_HastieAllshire1989` | STILL CURRENT | First systematic description of (TTAGGG)n at intrachromosomal positions; historical, still cited as origin of the ITS concept. |
| `subtelstruct_Nergadze2007` | STILL CURRENT | Telomerase-RNA retrotranscription as source of ITS; not superseded. |
| `subtelstruct_NergadzeITS2007` | STILL CURRENT | Primate-evolution ITS insertions at intrachromosomal break sites; specific mechanistic claim intact. |
| `subtelstruct_NergadzeITSReview2007` | STILL CURRENT | "Endings in the middle" ITS review; still most cited functional classification of ITS types. Rosas Bringas 2024 (PNAS) extends but does not supersede. |
| `subtelstruct_RuizHerrera2009` | STILL CURRENT | Review of telomeric repeats far from chromosome ends; remains a useful companion to NergadzeITSReview. |
| `subtelstruct_Riethman2005` | STILL CURRENT | Human subtelomere structure and variation review; still the most concise synthesis of the pre-T2T duplicon landscape. |
| `subtelstruct_Riethman2008` | STILL CURRENT | Allelic disparity at most distal 100 kb; the core observation (massive variation not captured by single reference) is validated, not superseded. |
| `subtelstruct_Young2020` | STILL CURRENT | BioNano optical mapping subtelomere population catalogue (154 genomes); remains the best pre-HPRC citation for population-scale subtelomere haplotype diversity. Superseded in sample size and resolution by the HPRC v2 analysis itself. |
| `subtelstruct_Logsdon2021` | STILL CURRENT | First gapless T2T chromosome (chr8); still primary citation for T2T-enabled subtelomere access. |
| `subtelstruct_Nurk2022` | STILL CURRENT | T2T-CHM13 complete genome; Gershman2022 is now bundled with this for repeat annotation; confirmed in Salsi 2026 as essential reference genome for D4Z4 landscape. |
| `subtelstruct_Grigorev2021` | STILL CURRENT | Telomere haplotype diversity (higher paternal inheritance of variants); still only population-scale telomere-repeat composition study. Karimian 2024 adds chromosome-end-specific length but does not address repeat-motif diversity. |
| `subtelstruct_Sholes2022` | STILL CURRENT but **BIB ERROR** | The content cited (three chromatin domains, CTCF-CpG boundary, TAR1 at 39/46 telomeres) matches the bioRxiv preprint at DOI 10.1101/2022.05.09.491186. However, the **bib entry has incorrect authors**: the actual preprint is by Dubocanin D., Sedeno Cortes A., Ranchalis J.E., Real T., Mallory B., Stergachis A.B. — not Sholes, Karimian, Gershman, Kelly, Timp. Recommend correcting author field to Dubocanin et al. and renaming key to `subtelstruct_Dubocanin2022`. The preprint remains unpublished as of 2026-05-17 (published_doi = NA). |
| `subtelstruct_Liao2023` | STILL CURRENT | Draft human pangenome reference (HPRC v1); directly cited in draft. |
| `Gershman2022` (in REFERENCES_v3) | STILL CURRENT | T2T repeat annotation including TERRA promoters at ~half of subtelomeres; Rodrigues 2024 extends this finding (more widespread than estimated) but the Gershman observation is the prior claim. |
| `Ma2024` (in REFERENCES_v3) | STILL CURRENT | CRISPR-induced chr4q/chr10q subtelomeric exchange reverts FSHD phenotype; already in reference universe. Should be cross-listed in topic-02 materials as it directly documents functional consequence of chr4/chr10 subtelomeric identity. |

## Section 3: NEW papers to add (STRONG relevance, 2023+)

---

**[rosasbringas2024its]** Rosas Bringas F.R., Yin Z., Yao Y., Boudeman J., Ollivaud S., Chang M. 2024. Interstitial telomeric sequences promote gross chromosomal rearrangement via multiple mechanisms. *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences*. PMID:39602274 / DOI:10.1073/pnas.2407314121

Claim it supports/contradicts: Topic 02 open question (lit review §"ITS function at domain boundaries"): "Recent work in model systems (PNAS 2024) shows that ITS promote gross chromosomal rearrangement via multiple mechanisms, strengthening the case for functional relevance." This is the specific paper cited there. The study uses *S. cerevisiae* to show ITS GCR rate increases exponentially with ITS length. Two distinct mechanisms: (1) Rap1 (telomere-repeat binding protein) impedes DNA replication at the ITS; (2) DSBs at or distal to ITS are repaired by de novo telomere addition rather than homologous recombination. A genome-wide screen identifies telomere maintenance, nucleotide excision repair, and transcription genes as ITS-GCR promoters — distinct from the classic GCR gene set, indicating ITS-induced rearrangements are mechanistically distinct.

Recommendation: **ADD** — this paper resolves the "PNAS 2024" citation currently unnamed in the lit review. Provides mechanistic basis for ITS instability relevant to the subtelomeric ITS population data in the current analysis (18,352 islands, 47.2% variant-dominant). Note that this is a yeast study; applicability to human subtelomeric ITS remains an open question as stated.

Bibtex stanza:
```bibtex
@article{rosasbringas2024its,
  author  = {Rosas Bringas, Fernando R and Yin, Ziqing and Yao, Yue and Boudeman, Jonathan and Ollivaud, Sandra and Chang, Michael},
  title   = {Interstitial telomeric sequences promote gross chromosomal rearrangement via multiple mechanisms},
  journal = {Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America},
  year    = {2024},
  volume  = {121},
  number  = {49},
  pages   = {e2407314121},
  doi     = {10.1073/pnas.2407314121},
  pmid    = {39602274}
}
```

**[karimian2024telomereend]** Karimian K., Groot A., Huso V., Kahidi R., Tan K.-T., Sholes S., Keener R., McDyer J.F., Alder J.K., Li H., Rechtsteiner A., Greider C.W. 2024. Human telomere length is chromosome end-specific and conserved across individuals. *Science*. PMID:38603523 / DOI:10.1126/science.ado0431

Claim it supports/contradicts: Topic 02 open question (§"TAR1 and telomere length homeostasis"): "Sholes et al.'s (2022) single-molecule data showed that telomeres adjacent to TAR1 elements are significantly longer and more variable in length than those without TAR1. This suggests TAR1 participates in telomerase access or processivity regulation." Karimian et al. 2024 establish the empirical basis for this hypothesis at population scale. Using a nanopore Telomere Profiling method, they show that chromosome-end-specific telomere length distributions are conserved across 147 individuals and present from birth (cord blood), with some chromosome ends consistently up to 6 kb longer or shorter than the genome average. The rank order of chromosome-end telomere lengths is maintained as telomeres shorten with age. This is the population-level confirmation that chromosome-arm-specific factors — of which TAR1 chromatin architecture (Dubocanin/Sholes 2022) is the leading candidate — durably determine telomere length set points.

Recommendation: **ADD** — directly tests and confirms the mechanistic prediction embedded in the topic-02 lit review. Provides a population anchor for the TAR1-telomere length hypothesis. Note the lead bioinformatics author is Heng Li; computational Telomere Profiling code is publicly available and compatible with HPRC v2 long-read data.

Bibtex stanza:
```bibtex
@article{karimian2024telomereend,
  author  = {Karimian, Kayarash and Groot, Aljona and Huso, Vienna and Kahidi, Ramin and Tan, Kar-Tong and Sholes, Samantha and Keener, Rebecca and McDyer, John F and Alder, Jonathan K and Li, Heng and Rechtsteiner, Andreas and Greider, Carol W},
  title   = {Human telomere length is chromosome end-specific and conserved across individuals},
  journal = {Science},
  year    = {2024},
  volume  = {384},
  number  = {6695},
  pages   = {533--539},
  doi     = {10.1126/science.ado0431},
  pmid    = {38603523}
}
```

**[salsi2026d4z4t2t]** Salsi V., Losi F., Pini S., Chiara M., Tupler R. 2026. Rethinking genomics of facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy in the telomere-to-telomere era: pitfalls in the hidden landscape of D4Z4 repeats. *European Journal of Human Genetics*. PMID:41535478 / DOI:10.1038/s41431-025-02000-x

Claim it supports/contradicts: Topic 02 open question (§"FSHD and subtelomeric disease"): "T2T-era work is now resolving D4Z4 arrays at full repeat resolution for the first time, and recent analyses flag pitfalls in diagnosing FSHD from short-read data due to complex D4Z4 repeat landscape in the T2T era." This is the specific paper that validates that statement. Using T2T-CHM13v2.0 as the query, the authors find D4Z4-like repeats with intact DUX4 ORFs or polyadenylation signals on at least 10 chromosomes beyond 4q35 and 10q26. Widely used FSHD diagnostic primer sets amplify these paralogous arrays. The study requires locus-resolved, repeat-aware, long-read approaches for accurate FSHD diagnosis — directly consistent with the HPRC v2 pangenome's discovery that D4Z4 DUX4 family copy counts range 0–22 across haplotypes (Extended Data Fig. 4c).

Recommendation: **ADD** — directly confirms and extends the D4Z4 claim in the existing lit review. Anchors the sentence about T2T-era pitfalls with a specific citation. This is the most directly actionable new reference for topic-02 content in the Nature draft regarding the C1 (4q/10q) community.

Bibtex stanza:
```bibtex
@article{salsi2026d4z4t2t,
  author  = {Salsi, Valentina and Losi, Francesca and Pini, Sara and Chiara, Matteo and Tupler, Rossella},
  title   = {Rethinking genomics of facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy in the telomere-to-telomere era: pitfalls in the hidden landscape of {D4Z4} repeats},
  journal = {European Journal of Human Genetics},
  year    = {2026},
  volume  = {34},
  number  = {3},
  pages   = {357--367},
  doi     = {10.1038/s41431-025-02000-x},
  pmid    = {41535478}
}
```

**[tardy2026fshdbeta]** Tardy C., Trani J.P., Murcia Pienkowski V., Morin L., Castro C., Souville L., Humbert C., Gerard L., Eudes N., Assoumani A., and 10 others, Magdinier F. 2026. Benchmarking long-read sequencing approaches to resolve facioscapulohumeral dystrophy locus complexity. *Brain*. PMID:41642686 / DOI:10.1093/brain/awag029

Claim it supports/contradicts: Report section 02_annotation.md (TAR1 and β-satellite subtelomeric architecture) and topic-02 open question (FSHD section). The paper shows that complex structural variants at 4q35 arise from intrachromosomal recombination between **LSau elements within D4Z4** and **distal subtelomeric β-satellite elements** — documenting that β-satellites (a distinct subtelomeric repeat family not previously prominent in topic 02's TAR1/ITS focus) actively mediate pathogenic rearrangements at the same chromosome ends where TAR1 and ITS reside. Variable deletions within the proximal D4Z4 region differ in breakpoint position among patients. Consistent with the general principle (Riethman 2008, Ambrosini 2007) that the subtelomeric repeat landscape drives structural variation and disease.

Recommendation: **ADD** — expands the D4Z4/FSHD discussion with the mechanism of duplicated allele formation and connects β-satellite elements to pathogenic recombination, a repeat class not previously foregrounded in topic-02. Relevant to the claim that no single reference can represent subtelomeric allelic diversity.

**[kanoh2023subtelomere]** Kanoh J. 2023. Subtelomeres: hotspots of genome variation. *Genes and Genetic Systems*. PMID:37648502 / DOI:10.1266/ggs.23-00049

Claim it supports/contradicts: General framing (topic 02, § era overview and § how this topic intersects adjacent topics). This is a concise 2023 review (5 pages) of subtelomere function and recent sequence variation discoveries from a fission yeast and broad eukaryotic perspective. It does not supersede any existing citation but provides a current snapshot of the field consensus suitable for citing alongside Riethman2005 when stating that subtelomeres are "hotspots of genome variation." Coverage includes: repeat diversity, gene content variation, evolutionary dynamics, and brief mention of T2T-era advances.

Recommendation: **ADD** as MEDIUM relevance background — a recent review that can replace or complement the decade-old Riethman2005 review in sentences establishing the general case that subtelomeres are highly variable. Score: MEDIUM (background framing).

**[rodrigues2024terra]** Rodrigues J., Alfieri R., Bione S., Azzalin C.M. 2024. TERRA ONTseq: a long-read-based sequencing pipeline to study the human telomeric transcriptome. *RNA*. PMID:38777382 / DOI:10.1261/rna.079906.123

Claim it supports/contradicts: Topic-02 lit review §"ITS function at domain boundaries" and the Gershman2022 claim about TERRA promoters. The paper uses long-read nanopore sequencing to show that (a) the **vast majority** of human telomeres produce TERRA (not just ~half as inferred by Gershman et al. 2022 from CHM13); (b) TERRA TSS regions are found in >half of subtelomeres, downstream from 29-bp repeat-related sequences that are **more widespread than previously estimated**; (c) a novel TERRA promoter is identified at chr7q. This directly extends Gershman2022's TERRA promoter observation and the topic-02 structural landscape (29-bp CpG repeats as a subtelomeric promoter element adjacent to the terminal repeat).

Recommendation: **ADD** as MEDIUM relevance — updates the TERRA promoter claim in Gershman2022 (cited in topic-02 lit review), provides a higher-resolution landscape of the subtelomeric transcriptional boundary, relevant to the chromatin domain structure described in the Dubocanin/Sholes 2022 preprint. Does not alter any specific numerical claim in the draft.

Bibtex stanza:
```bibtex
@article{rodrigues2024terra,
  author  = {Rodrigues, Joana and Alfieri, Roberta and Bione, Silvia and Azzalin, Claus M},
  title   = {{TERRA ONTseq}: a long-read-based sequencing pipeline to study the human telomeric transcriptome},
  journal = {RNA},
  year    = {2024},
  volume  = {30},
  number  = {8},
  pages   = {955--966},
  doi     = {10.1261/rna.079906.123},
  pmid    = {38777382}
}
```

**[kim2025koreansvs]** Kim J., Park J.L., Yang J.O., Kim S., Joe S., Park G., Hwang T., Cho M.-J., Lee S., Lee J.-E., Park J.-H., Yeo M.-K., Kim S.-Y. 2025. Highly accurate Korean draft genomes reveal structural variation highlighting human telomere evolution. *Nucleic Acids Research*. PMID:39778865 / DOI:10.1093/nar/gkae1294

Claim it supports/contradicts: Topic-02 open question (§"Reference-free population-scale structural annotation") and report section 02_annotation.md (subtelomeric SV mechanisms). This paper generates ~20x HiFi long-read assemblies from three Korean individuals, identifies 19 large subtelomeric SVs (≥5 kb), and performs manual investigation of their DNA damage-repair signatures to infer the repair mechanisms (NHEJ, MMEJ, fork stalling and template switching). This is the most mechanistically detailed population-scale characterisation of subtelomeric SV formation mechanisms to date in a non-HPRC cohort. Confirms that subtelomeric regions are enriched for SVs (vs. non-subtelomeric regions), consistent with Riethman2008.

Recommendation: **ADD** as MEDIUM relevance — provides mechanistic SV data complementing the structural description in the lit review. Useful for the open question about what drives subtelomeric SV formation in human populations.

Bibtex stanza:

## Section 4: CONTRADICTIONS

No papers in the 2023–2026 search window directly contradict claims made in the Nature draft or the existing topic-02 lit review.

Two papers require a **nuance note** rather than contradiction:

1. **Rodrigues et al. 2024 vs Gershman et al. 2022**: Gershman stated TERRA promoters (29-bp CpG repeats) are "present at roughly half of subtelomeres." Rodrigues et al. 2024 report TERRA TSS regions "in more than half of human subtelomeres" with the 29-bp motif being "more widespread than previously estimated." The direction is the same (both say ~half or more); Rodrigues extends upward. No contradiction, but reviewers may ask for updated language.

2. **Rosas Bringas et al. 2024 (yeast) vs human ITS claim**: The topic-02 lit review states "Whether this applies to the subtelomeric ITS in human germline or somatic cells is an open question." The Rosas Bringas paper is a yeast study. It does not contradict the human claim but also does not confirm it — the species gap is real and should remain explicit in the draft.

---

## Section 5: Search audit trail

### Tools used
1. **mcp__claude_ai_PubMed__search_articles** (PubMed MCP) — queries via NCBI E-utilities; date filter: 2023/01/01–2026/12/31
2. **mcp__claude_ai_PubMed__get_article_metadata** — metadata retrieval for specific PMIDs
3. **mcp__claude_ai_bioRxiv__get_preprint** — bioRxiv preprint metadata lookup (DOI-specific)
4. **OpenAlex API** (openalex-database skill, `search_works` with `publication_year: 2023-2026`) — cross-validation

### Date range applied
2023-01-01 to 2026-05-17 for all novel-paper searches. Extended to 2022 only for checking publication status of `subtelstruct_Sholes2022` (DOI 10.1101/2022.05.09.491186, verified still unpublished).

### Total hits before relevance filter
Approximately 90 unique articles retrieved across all queries (de-duplicated by PMID/DOI).

### Hits after relevance filter
7 new papers: 5 STRONG (rosasbringas2024its, karimian2024telomereend, salsi2026d4z4t2t, tardy2026fshdbeta, rodrigues2024terra) + 2 MEDIUM (kanoh2023subtelomere, kim2025koreansvs). Note: Ma2024 and Gershman2022 were confirmed already in REFERENCES_v3.bib.
